title: Fixing Incomplete Git Tag Name Validation

statement.markdown: Write a function `isValidTag` that validates a given Git tag name. A valid Git tag name must be at least 1 character long, but not longer than 40 characters. It must start with a letter or a digit and can only contain letters, digits, hyphens, and dots. The function should return `True` if the tag name is valid, and `False` otherwise.
statement.inputFormat: A string representing the tag name to be validated.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the tag name is valid.
statement.notes: Time complexity should be O(n), where n is the length of the tag name.

examples.explanation: The tag name starts with a letter and contains only valid characters
The tag name exceeds the maximum allowed length
The tag name starts with a hyphen

hints.text: Use regular expressions to match the pattern of a valid tag name

editorial.title: Editorial for Fixing Incomplete Git Tag Name Validation
editorial.intuition: The key insight is to use a regular expression to match the pattern of a valid tag name.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve manually checking every possible character in the tag name, which would result in a time complexity of O(n^2).
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution can be achieved by using the `^` character to match the start of the string, and the `[a-zA-Z0-9.-]` pattern to match any letter, digit, hyphen, or dot.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using the `^` character and the `[a-zA-Z0-9.-]` pattern in a regular expression to validate the tag name.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The regular expression `^[a-zA-Z0-9][a-zA-Z0-9.-]{0,38}$` matches any string that starts with a letter or digit and contains at most 39 valid characters.
editorial.dryRun: For example, the tag name `v1.2.3` would match the regular expression and be considered valid.
commonMistakes: Failing to include the `^` character to match the start of the string
Including invalid characters in the pattern
